coredata.dc:title: Concurrent synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots for cell imaging and ZnO@nitrogen-doped carbon sheets for photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Atchudan, Raji
Edison, Thomas Nesakumar Jebakumar Immanuel
Perumal, Suguna
Karthik, Namachivayam
Karthikeyan, Dhanapalan
Shanmugam, Mani
Lee, Yong Rok
coredata.dc:description: The hybrid composite of ZnO nanoparticles decorated nitrogen-doped graphitic carbon sheets (ZnO@N-C) alongside nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were synthesized by the direct hydrothermal method of peach fruit juice and ZnO nanoparticles (ZnO NPs). The synthesized ZnO@N-C hybrid composite and N-CDs were thoroughly characterized by various physicochemical techniques including powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD), attenuated total reflectance Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectroscopy, field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) with energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Raman spectroscopy, ultraviolet-visible (UV-vis) spectroscopy, and fluorescence spectroscopy. The ZnO NPs were dispersed in the graphitic carbon structure with an average size of 25±5nm. The chemical composition of C, O, N, and Zn within the ZnO@N-C hybrid composite exhibits enough graphitization. Methylene blue (MB)-degradation was estimated utilizing the ZnO@N-C hybrid composite, a maximum degradation efficiency of >95% was achieved in a neutral aqueous medium within 60min under UV-light irradiation. This maximum efficiency allows estimating the contribution of the heterogeneous and homogeneity of ZnO@N-C hybrid composite which is responsible for the MB-degradation. Moreover, the economic natural biosource or bio-waste was employed for the synthesis of ZnO@N-C hybrid composite and displays an excellent photocatalytic degradation under UV-light irradiation which is an alternate for other carbon-based metal oxides. In addition, the resulting N-CDs were utilized as a fluorescence probe for cellular imaging, owing to their tremendous properties such as bright fluorescence with high quantum yield, excellent water solubility, and good biocompatibility. The N-CDs displays a multicolour fluorescence based on their changing of excitation wavelength and these multicolour fluorescence emissions offered a multicolour cellular imaging which could be applicable for real biological system in the near future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Peach fruit
Hydrothermal method
Carbon dots
Cell imaging
Photocatalyst
Methylene blue

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of N-CDs and ZnO@N-C hybrid composite
Cell culture, imaging, and viability assay
Characterization of the synthesized ZnO@N-C hybrid composite
Photocatalytic degradation activities of the synthesized ZnO@N-C hybrid composite
Cytotoxicity and cell imaging of the synthesized N-CDs
Fluorescence prolong stability of the synthesized N-CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Korean Ministry of Education, Science, and Technology
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Education, Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the Nano Material Technology Development Program of the Korean National Research Foundation (NRF) funded by the Korean Ministry of Education, Science, and Technology (grant number 2012M3A7B4049675 ). This work was also supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Priority Research Centers Program (grant number 2014R1A6A1031189 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the synthesis and applications of N-CDs and ZnO@N-C hybrid composite.
XRD pattern (a) and FT-IR spectrum (b) of synthesized ZnO@N-C hybrid composite.
FESEM images with different magnification (a-d), elemental mapping of carbon (e), oxygen (f), nitrogen (g), zinc (h) and corresponding EDX spectrum (i) of synthesized ZnO@N-C hybrid composite.
HRTEM images with different magnification (a-e) and SAED pattern (f) of synthesized ZnO@N-C hybrid composite.
(a) XPS survey scan spectrum, (b), (c), (d) and (e) XPS high-resolution spectra of C 1s, N 1s, O 1s and Zn 2p for synthesized ZnO@N-C hybrid composite.
(a), (b) HRTEM images with different magnification and (c) particle size distribution histogram of the N-CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorbance spectrum, (b) excitation and emission fluorescence spectra, (c) excitation dependent fluorescence emission spectra and (d) bar diagram for excitation dependent maximum emission wavelength of the N-CDs. Inset (a): photographs of resulted N-CDs in water under daylight and UV light (λex =365nm).

UV–vis spectra of MB-degradation in the presence of (a) ZnO@N-C hybrid composite depends on irradiation time, (b) MB-degradation (%) at different irradiation time (inset b: photographs of MB aqueous solution before and after degradation), (c) irradiation time-dependent first order kinetics plot of ln(At/A0) and (d) plausible mechanism for the MB-degradation.

Confocal fluorescent microscopy images of Clone 9 hepatocytes in the presence and absence of N-CDs for 24h incubation time at three different excitation wavelength such as 405 (Blue), 488 (Green) and 555nm (Red). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The fluorescence spectra and photographs of the synthesized N-CDs solution at different storage time.
Comparison of photocatalytic activities from previous reported work with ZnO nanoparticle based composite on MB degradation.

head.ce:title: Concurrent synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots for cell imaging and ZnO@nitrogen-doped carbon sheets for photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, Gyeongsan 38541, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Chemistry, Kyungpook National University, Daegu 41566, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Arignar Anna College of Arts and Science, Krishnagiri 635001, Tamilnadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Arignar Anna College of Arts and Science

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Institute of Aeronautical Engineering, Dundigal, Hyderabad - 500043, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The hybrid composite of ZnO nanoparticles decorated nitrogen-doped graphitic carbon sheets (ZnO@N-C) alongside nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were synthesized by the direct hydrothermal method of peach fruit juice and ZnO nanoparticles (ZnO NPs). The synthesized ZnO@N-C hybrid composite and N-CDs were thoroughly characterized by various physicochemical techniques including powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD), attenuated total reflectance Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectroscopy, field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) with energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Raman spectroscopy, ultraviolet-visible (UV-vis) spectroscopy, and fluorescence spectroscopy. The ZnO NPs were dispersed in the graphitic carbon structure with an average size of 25±5nm. The chemical composition of C, O, N, and Zn within the ZnO@N-C hybrid composite exhibits enough graphitization. Methylene blue (MB)-degradation was estimated utilizing the ZnO@N-C hybrid composite, a maximum degradation efficiency of >95% was achieved in a neutral aqueous medium within 60min under UV-light irradiation. This maximum efficiency allows estimating the contribution of the heterogeneous and homogeneity of ZnO@N-C hybrid composite which is responsible for the MB-degradation. Moreover, the economic natural biosource or bio-waste was employed for the synthesis of ZnO@N-C hybrid composite and displays an excellent photocatalytic degradation under UV-light irradiation which is an alternate for other carbon-based metal oxides. In addition, the resulting N-CDs were utilized as a fluorescence probe for cellular imaging, owing to their tremendous properties such as bright fluorescence with high quantum yield, excellent water solubility, and good biocompatibility. The N-CDs displays a multicolour fluorescence based on their changing of excitation wavelength and these multicolour fluorescence emissions offered a multicolour cellular imaging which could be applicable for real biological system in the near future.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, environmental pollution, especially water pollution is a critical problem in the world. Organic dyes and their effluents are one of the main sources of water pollution which is commonly escape from the traditional wastewater treatment plants including the mining, textile, paper printing, food processing, cosmetics, pharmaceutical and leather industries [1–5]. Water pollution containing methylene blue (MB) is threatening to the ecological balance even the survival of human due to their non-biodegradability, toxicity, carcinogenicity, and bioaccumulation. Therefore, it is an essential to find out effective methods for the removal of dyes in the water [6–8]. Recent days, different techniques including ion exchange, adsorption, membrane filtration, electrochemical treatment, and photocatalytic degradation have been developed to remove the excessive MB from the industrial waste water. The photocatalytic degradation is a preferred approach for the degradation of organic waste because of their high efficiency and low-cost over other methods [9]. Many semiconducting materials used as a photocatalyst for the degradation of organic pollutants. Among these various materials, zinc oxide (ZnO) is used as effective photocatalysts because of its large band gap (3.3eV), and other beneficial characteristics such as larger binding energy (60eV), an efficient absorption of a large fraction of the solar energy with low cost. Thus, the ZnO can be used as a competitive photocatalyst material for potential uses in the removal of organic dyes from wastewater with high efficiency [10–12]. In addition, graphene-based ZnO composites significantly enhance the photocatalytic performance due to their unique properties such as excellent mechanical strength, extraordinary large surface area, and facile modification. However, the graphene-based materials possess poor solubility in water and less functional groups have limited their application in the wastewater treatment and thus the number of workers have been carried out to modify graphene oxide with multifunctional materials to break these restrictions [13]. In general, the natural biosource derived from carbon sheets has a vast number of functional groups on the basal and edge of the carbon layers (sheets).

On the other hand, carbon dots (CDs) are a recent member of the renowned carbon-based nanomaterials family have attracted a significant interest from various fields such as optoelectronic, photocatalysis, electrocatalysis, biosensing, bioimaging and drug delivery owing to their unique properties including excellent photoluminescence, good biocompatibility, good photostability, excellent water solubility, tunable surface functionalities as well as the abundant and inexpensive nature [14–17]. Moreover, the nitrogen- and oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of the CDs have intensely enhanced the solubility as well as optoelectronic properties which lead to the wide applications effectively. In general, CDs and functional CDs are synthesized by arc-discharge, laser ablation, ultra-sonication, microwave, solvothermal and hydrothermal methods. By contrast, the hydrothermal method is a facile, green, and an efficient approach. Currently, natural biosources/biowastes are extensively used for the preparation of functional CDs by the hydrothermal method which has gained popularity due to the abundance of these carbon sources, low cost, and environmental friendliness. In this regard, the preparation of high quality functionalized CDs from a suitable biosource is an essential.

Herein, the ZnO@N-C hybrid composite composed of ZnO NPs-decorated nitrogen-doped graphitic-carbon sheets and nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were synthesized simultaneously from the green natural source of peach fruit and ZnO NPs by the simple hydrothermal method. It was employed to determine the photocatalytic activity on (MB)-degradation under UV-light. The homogenous distribution of ZnO NPs on the nitrogen-doped graphitic-carbon promote the photocatalytic activity. Importantly, These nitrogen-doped graphitic carbon layers were derived from inexpensive natural bio-source and because of the increased light absorption as well as the reduced charge recombination it enhances the photocatalytic efficiency. Moreover, the enhancement of photocatalytic activity was credited to the synergistic effect between ZnO NPs and nitrogen-doped graphitic carbon layers. In addition, the resulting N-CDs were utilized as a multicolour fluorescence probe for cellular imaging without any chemical modification based on their multicolour fluorescence emission with good biocompatibility. Hence, the simple method may provide a new strategy for fabricating the ZnO@N-C hybrid composite for dye degradation and N-CDs with superior optical properties promote addressing in bio-imaging applications.

The peach fruits were obtained from the Yeungnam University Campus, Gyeongsan, Republic of Korea. Zinc powder (Zn P), aqueous ammonia (25%), phosphate buffered saline (PBS), p-formaldehyde, quinine sulphate and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, Republic of Korea. 30-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT) purchased from Generay Biotech, Shanghai, China. Clone 9 hepatocytes were obtained from ATCC, CRL-1439, Manassas, VA, USA. All the chemicals were used as received. The deionized water was used for the preparation of samples throughout this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N-CDs and ZnO@N-C hybrid composite
ce:section.ce:para: The ZnO NPs were synthesized utilizing Zn P as per our previous report [18]. Both the N-CDs and ZnO@N-C hybrid composites were simultaneously synthesized through a hydrothermal method. In a typical synthesis procedure, 30mL of peach juice and 200mg of Zn NPs water were taken into a beaker followed by 1mL of aqueous ammonia. The mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, subsequently heated at 180°C for 12h. After that, the final mixture was naturally cooled to room temperature, filtered, and washed with deionized water several times. The residue was dried at 70°C for overnight. The obtained dark brown material denoted as ZnO@N-C hybrid composites. The obtained filtrate shows a bright fluorescence under UV-light (365nm) and denoted as N-CDs. The N-CDs with bright fluorescence was further applied for multicolour cellular imaging and the ZnO@N-C hybrid composite was employed for the MB-degradation which are clearly demonstrated in Fig. 1 .

The photocatalytic degradation was examined by degradation of MB in a neutral aqueous medium at room temperature under irradiation of UV lamp. For this, the absorbance intensity of MB solution was fixed around 1.6. Typically, 20mg of the synthesized ZnO@N-C hybrid composite was dispersed into a 50mL of MB aqueous solution in a glass beaker. The suspension was placed at 100mm distance from UV light source and was irradiated with UV light under mild stirring until the decolouration of MB solution. At 15min irradiation time intervals, 2mL of suspensions were collected and absorbance was measured until zero value attained for the determination of the MB degradation with UV–vis spectrophotometer. The percentage of degradation and kinetic rate of degradation of MB dye was calculated in presence of synthesized ZnO@N-C hybrid composite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell culture, imaging, and viability assay
ce:section.ce:para: The Clone 9 hepatocytes were cultured on coverslips in 6-well plates to examine the fluorescent confocal imaging. The cells were incubated in the presence and absence of N-CDs in a humidified chamber at 37°C for 24h which is denoted as cell staining. After staining, the cells were fixed with 4% p-formaldehyde and washed thrice with PBS. The stained cells were examined by the confocal fluorescence microscopy using different filters such as blue, green, red and also bright field (BF). The toxicity of the synthesized N-CDs against Clone 9 hepatocytes was examined by the MTT assay. Typically, the Clone 9 hepatocytes were cultured in F-12K medium containing 10% FBS in a 96-well plate at 37°C under 5% CO2 atmosphere until confluence, followed by the treatment with different concentrations of N-CDs (0–100μgmL−1) for 24h. The MTT solution (5mg MTT/mL in PBS) was added to each well of the microtiter plate and incubated for 4h at 37°C in a dark humidified chamber. Finally, purple coloured formazan crystals formed only by living cells. The formazan crystals were dissolved in DMSO and absorbance was recorded at 540nm using a microtiter plate reader.

The crystallinity, as well as graphitization, were determined by powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) analysis which was performed using powder diffractometer PANalytical X’Pert3 MRD employing monochromatized Cu Kα radiation (λ=1.54Å) at 40kV and 30mA and was recorded in the range from 10 to 90° (2θ). For the measurement of the sample, the powder sample was packed on top of an appropriate quartz substrate which belongs to XRD analysis. Raman spectrum was recorded using a Thermo Scientific DXR SmartRaman spectrometer with laser excitation at 532nm to determine the graphitization of the sample. Functionalization of synthesized materials was determined by Attenuated total reflectance Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectroscopy using Perkin Elmer Spectrum Two with the addition of 16 scans at a resolution of 16cm−1. The surface morphology and elemental compositions were revealed by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) equipped with energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy (Hitachi S-4800). The morphology and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns were determined by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) with accelerating voltage of 200kV (FEI-Tecnai TF-20). For HRTEM analysis, a drop of the diluted sample solution was placed on the carbon-coated copper grid and was naturally dried. The chemical compositions were revealed via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements using Thermo Scientific K-Alpha. The non-monochromatic Al Kα line at 180–200W was used as the primary excitation. CasaXPS software was used to deconvolute the high-resolution XPS spectra. The optical properties were evaluated by the ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra (OPTIZEN 3220UV spectrophotometer) and fluorescence excitation as well as emission spectra (Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer). The slit width as 5nm and scan speed as 240nm/min were fixed for the fluorescence measurement. Cellular imaging was performed using confocal laser scanning microscopy ×400, LSM700, Carl Zeiss, Oberkochen, Germany.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the synthesized ZnO@N-C hybrid composite
ce:section.ce:para: The wide-angle PXRD pattern of the ZnO@N-C hybrid composite is shown in Fig. 2 (a) and the pattern is corresponding to the wurtzite phase of hexagonal ZnO which matches well with the standard JCPDS Card No. 36–1451 [19,20]. The lattice distance of Zn NPs within the ZnO@N-C hybrid composite is ca. 0.28 and 0.25nm based on the 100 and 101 diffraction planes, respectively. In addition, the small and board peak observed at 22.2° (2θ) corresponds to the C(002) plane of the typical hexagonal graphitic structure [21]. Moreover, the ZnO diffraction peaks are prominent in the pattern of the ZnO@N-C hybrid composite, suggesting that the ZnO NPs may have a high degree of crystallinity. The Raman spectrum of the ZnO@N-C hybrid composite is given in Fig. S1 in which the spectrum exhibit two predominant peaks at 1360 and 1590cm−1, it can be assigned to disordered sp3 hybridized carbon denoted as a D band and sp2 hybridized graphite carbon denoted as a G band, respectively [22,23]. The intensity ratio of D to G band (ID/IG) is ca. 0.80 which demonstrates the resulting nitrogen-doped graphitic carbon layers possess a moderate degree of graphitization [24]. In addition, the minute peaks are observed below 800cm−1 in the Raman spectrum which is the characteristic peak of the hexagonal wurtzite phase (ZnO NPs). The characteristic ZnO NPs peaks are diminished might be due to the carbon layers covered on the surface of the ZnO NPs.

ATR-FTIR spectroscopy is the powerful tool to observe the surface functionalization of the ZnO@N-C hybrid composite. As shown in Fig. 2b, the hydrogen bonded hydroxyl group (–OH) stretching vibration band centered around 3250cm−1 and C−H as-symmetric stretching vibration at 2928cm−1 for the synthesized ZnO@N-C hybrid composite. The band at 1596cm−1 corresponds to the in-plane vibrations sp2-hybridized graphitic honeycomb carbon structure (CC). The CN heterocycles stretching vibration and −OH bending (C−OH) vibration observed at 1436 and 1372cm−1, respectively, which strongly indicates the presence of oxygen-contained and nitrogen-contained molecules over the carbon layers. The vibration band between 1276 and 1026cm−1 corresponds to the epoxy (COC) functional groups [25–27]. Furthermore, the band at 458cm−1 observed in the spectrum can be assigned to ZnO vibration within ZnO framework which suggesting that the synthesized materials exhibit the ZnO NPs in the nitrogen-doped carbon structure with only the physical adsorption without any chemical bonding.

The surface morphology with elemental composition and structural morphology with crystallization/graphitization were assessed using FESEM with EDX elemental mapping and HRTEM with SAED techniques. Fig. 3 represent the FESEM images with EDX mapping of the synthesized ZnO@N-C hybrid composite. The small nanoparticles were evenly distributed on the surface of the carbon layer which is vividly seen in Fig. 3a–c. Owing to the ZnO NPs, the surface of the resulting carbon layer was little rougher than common un-doped carbon layers. The ZnO NPs are evenly distributed on the graphene layers which is further confirmed by the EDX mapping images. The FESEM image (Fig. 3d) and the corresponding EDX mapping are shown in (Fig. 3e–h), the Zn and N are consistently distributed on the matrix. On the other hand, the EDX pattern of ZnO@N-C hybrid composite shown in Fig. 3i asserts the existence of carbon (C), oxygen (O) and zinc (Zn) elements. The carbon signal should mainly originate from peach juice derived carbon sheets, whereas the oxygen peak should come from the Zn-O rather than residual oxygen-containing functional groups over the carbon sheets [28]. The absence of any other elements in the EDX spectrum suggests that the synthesis processes did not bring any impurities for the ZnO@N-C hybrid composite.

The HRTEM was used to further confirm the presence of ZnO NPs as well as carbon layers and also their crystallization as well as graphitization in the synthesized ZnO@N-C hybrid composite. The HRTEM images (Fig. 4 ) apparently show the combination of carbon layers and ZnO NPs. The ZnO NPs consistently appear over the carbon layers without major aggregation and they have a narrow size distribution. The HRTEM images (Fig. 4d and e) are clearly demonstrate the lattice fringe of ZnO NPs and the lattice spacing values are ∼0.26 and 0.28nm corresponds to the (101) and (100) crystalline plane of the ZnO phase, respectively. [29]. In addition, SAED pattern Fig. 4(f) shows the diffraction rings of ZnO NPs which clearly demonstrate the smaller size of ZnO NPs with highly crystalline in nature. Further, SAED pattern coincides with the XRD result.

The chemical composition of the synthesized ZnO@N-C hybrid composite revealed by XPS analysis. XPS survey scan spectrum of ZnO@N-C hybrid composite clearly reveals the presence of C, O, N and Zn from the various orbital levels (Fig. 5 a). The high-resolution C1s core level spectrum is shown in Fig. 5b which was deconvoluted into four peaks at 284.68, 285.58, 287.48, and 291.48eV corresponding to the non-oxygenated ring C (CC/CC), C associated with OH/N (COH/CN), carbonyl/carboxyl (CO/OCOH) and epoxy (COC) functional groups, respectively [30,31]. The high-resolution N1s spectrum (Fig. 5c) by deconvolution gives two peaks at 399.68 and 401.18eV assigned to pyridinic-N (CNC) and graphitic-N (C3–N) functional groups, respectively [32]. Three functional groups can be revealed from the deconvoluted curves of O1s which are related to the carbonyl (CO), epoxy/hydroxyl/metal framework oxygen (COC/COH/ZnO), and surface adsorbed water molecules (HOH) at the peak position of 531.18, 532.48, and 534.88eV, respectively (Fig. 5d). The deconvolution of Zn2p resolved into two distinct peaks at the binding energy of 1022.18 and 1045.18eV corresponding to Zn2p3/2 and Zn2p1/2 levels, respectively. The peak separation value was of 23eV, which is in good agreement with the energy splitting of ZnO as in an earlier report [33]. Furthermore, the XPS results revealed that the ZnO NPs was physically bounded and N-atom was chemically bonded in the nitrogen-doped carbon layers which are consistent with the above ATR-FTIR spectroscopy results. According to the aforementioned characterization, the synthesized ZnO@N-C hybrid composite is well composed of C, O, N, and Zn components with a high degree of crystallization/graphitization. The well characterized ZnO@N-C hybrid composite was utilized as a photocatalyst for the degradation of MB dye.

Fig. 6 show the HRTEM images and the size distribution of the synthesized N-CDs. The images show that the N-CDs are monodispersed, nearly spherical shaped with an average diameter of about 4nm and mainly distributed in the range of 3–5nm. The HRTEM image of the N-CDs (Fig. 6b) reveals the high crystallinity with a lattice spacing of 0.21nm which corresponds to the (100) diffraction plane of typical graphite [34]. Structural property of N-CDs was investigated by the XRD analysis. Fig. S2 depicts the wide-angle PXRD pattern of N-CDs where a broad peak around 21.9° (2θ) assigned to the (002) diffraction plane of typical graphite. The pattern suggests that synthesized N-CDs exhibits the moderate crystallines phases of carbon structure [35]. The ATR-FTIR spectroscopy was carried out to identify the various functional groups present in synthesized N-CDs and the detailed results are shown in Fig. S3. The characteristic functional groups such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, nitrogen, and epoxy were obviously observed in the ATR-FTIR spectrum of the N-CDs [36]. Therefore, the water solubility of the N-CDs could possibly be attributed to the hydrophilic functional groups on the surfaces of the N-CDs (see the ESI for the detailed discussion).

The optical properties of the synthesized N-CDs were revealed by UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy analysis. The UV–vis spectrum of N-CDs in Fig. 7 a shows a prominent absorption peak around 270nm and shoulder peaks around 305nm which are attributed to the π–π* transition of the in-plane sp2-hybridized honeycomb-like carbon (CC) and n–π* transition of the oxygen-containing or nitrogen-containing functional groups (CO/CN) in the N-CDs, respectively [37,38]. Inset Fig. 7a displays the photographs of the aqueous N-CDs solution under daylight as well as UV light (λex =365nm). The aqueous N-CDs solution emits the bright blue fluorescence under UV light. Fig. 7b shows the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of synthesized N-CDs. A predominant peak around 345nm can be observed in the fluorescence excitation spectrum. The fluorescence emission peak was observed at a wavelength of 421nm under the excitation wavelength of 340nm. Further, the fluorescence emission properties of synthesized N-CDs were examined by the variation of excitation wavelength. The excitation-dependent fluorescence emission spectra (Fig. 7c) shows the emission intensity of the N-CDs varied by the varying of the excitation wavelength. The fluorescence emission intensity was increased up to the excitation wavelength of 340nm beyond that gradually decreased. A maximum emission peak centered at 421 by the excitation of 340nm with a stock shift of 81nm. Besides, the fluorescence emission wavelength increases (red-shifts) with increasing the excitation wavelength from 290 to 420nm (Fig. 7d). This fluorescence behaviour is may be attributed to the distribution of surface emissive trap sites [39–41]. Quantum yield (QY) is important for the fluorescence properties and the QY of the synthesized N-CDs was calculated by measuring the fluorescence intensity in an aqueous dispersion by using quinine sulphate as a standard reference [42]. The QY of N-CDs was measured at an excitation wavelength of 340nm, and the yield was around 7.5%. Hence, this QY strongly suggests that the synthesized N-CDs exhibits a bright fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic degradation activities of the synthesized ZnO@N-C hybrid composite
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic activity of the synthesized ZnO@N-C hybrid composite has been explored by degradation of MB in an aqueous medium under illumination of UV, the results are shown in Fig. 8 . As shown in Fig. 8a, the absorbance intensity of MB is decreased with increasing the irradiation time and the absorbance intensity was nearly zero at the time of irradiation about 60min. Further, Fig. 8b demonstrates the time-dependent degradation attainment curve for MB with ZnO@N-C hybrid composite. The degradation efficiency is about 95% when extending the irradiation time to 60min. Reactions followed pseudo-first order kinetics (Fig. 8c), and initial rates determined from the linear portion of the reaction profiles. The rate constant (k) is calculated as 0.0456min−1 (7.6×10−4 s−1) with the R2 value of 0.999, which is the higher value compared to the synthesized ZnO NPs on MB degradation [18]. In order to justify the superior activity of the synthesized ZnO@N-C hybrid composite, the present study was compared with the recent reports of MB degradation by ZnO based composites and results were shown in Table 1 [43−49]. This superior photocatalytic activity might be due to the monodisperse of ZnO NPs on the surface of the nitrogen-doped as well as hydroxyl containing carbon layers derived from a natural green source which resulting ZnO@N-C hybrid composite. These result revealed that the synthesized ZnO@N-C hybrid composite is a potential candidate for photocatalytic degradation of MB under UV light irradiation in a neutral aqueous medium. A possible mechanism of photocatalytic degradation of MB over the ZnO@N-C hybrid composite has been proposed based on the photocatalytic studies as shown in Fig. 8d. Upon the UV light irradiation, the electrons are excited in the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB) of ZnO NPs, thus creating holes (h+) in the VB. The generated electron transferred to the CB of carbon layers and carbon layer eject the photogenerated electron which reacts with oxygen (O2) to generate super oxide anion radicals (O2 •−). The high separation rate of h+ easily reacts with water molecules (H2O) to produce the hydroxyl radicals (•OH). Both the formation of the above radicals leads to degradation of the adsorbed MB dye over the ZnO@N-C hybrid composite. The efficient electron transfer process with carbon layers as electron collector and transporter is responsible for the enhanced photocatalytic performance of the ZnO@N-C hybrid composite under irradiation of UV-light. The supporting carbon layer prevents the recombination of electron–hole pairs over the semiconducting ZnO NPs [50–52]. Hence, the enhancement of photocatalytic performance was ascribed to the synergistic effect between ZnO NPs and carbon layers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity and cell imaging of the synthesized N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of the synthesized N-CDs against Clone 9 hepatocytes was evaluated by the standard MTT assay. The cell viability was examined at different concentrations of N-CDs which are shown in Fig. S4. The N-CDs did not show any significant toxicity on Clone 9 hepatocytes even at a concentration of 100μg/mL. The viability is observed above 97% with an incubation time of 24h. Hence, the result indicating that the synthesized N-CDs was biocompatible and it can be utilized as a promising candidate for safe bioimaging applications under physiological conditions [53]. Cellular imaging of N-CDs treated and untreated Clone 9 hepatocytes were captured with different excitation wavelengths of 405 (blue), 488 (green) and 555nm (red) laser pulses using confocal laser scanning microscopy which is shown in Fig. 9 . There is no fluorescence observed in the untreated Clone 9 hepatocytes (control). The cells uptake the N-CDs and thus display the multicolour emission in Clone 9 hepatocytes. In addition, the emitting the multicolour fluorescence without any morphological defects of cells, endorsing the biocompatibility of the N-CDs. These results confirmed that the N-CDs show stable fluorescence, it can act as a potential candidate for multicolour cell imaging and biological fields without any chemical modifications [54].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence prolong stability of the synthesized N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The prolong stability of the synthesized N-CDs was examined by fluorescence spectroscopy and UV light irradiation photograph analysis. Fig. 10 a shows the naked eye photographic images of an aqueous solution of the synthesized N-CDs at the time of experiment and 6-months later under daylight as well as UV light with an excitation wavelength of 365nm. The photographs of an aqueous solution of N-CDs is colourless under daylight but show bright blue fluorescence under UV light. The bright blue fluorescence can be observed in the naked eyes without major differences under UV light irradiation even after 6-months of storage which established the stability of the synthesized N-CDs. Further, the fluorescence stability was confirmed by fluorescence spectra. Fig. 10b shows the fluorescence spectra of the synthesized N-CDs at an excitation wavelength of 340nm before and after 6-months of storage. The spectra did not show any major significance in the fluorescence intensities of both before and after 6-months storage of N-CDs owing to the prolonged fluorescence stability of the N-CDs. Thus, these results strongly suggested that the synthesized N-CDs possess excellent fluorescence stability for a long time.

In summary, a simple green hydrothermal method was employed to synthesize the ZnO@N-C hybrid composite and N-CDs concurrently using a peach fruit juice with ZnO NPs which is different from the traditional synthesis method. The ZnO@N-C hybrid composite and N-CDs characteristics were examined by PXRD, FTIR, FESEM with EDX, HRTEM, XPS, UV-vis, and fluorescence spectroscopy techniques. The ZnO NPs was consistently anchored on the surface of the nitrogen-doped carbon layers with the composition of C, O, N and Zn within the ZnO@N-C hybrid composite. The well constructed ZnO@N-C hybrid composite was utilized as a photocatalyst for the degradation of MB. The photocatalytic degradation efficiency of ZnO@N-C hybrid composite on MB obeys the pseudo-first-order kinetics. The degradation rate constant (k) of MB dye with ZnO@N-C hybrid composite was 0.0456min−1. The results showed that the synthesized ZnO@N-C hybrid composite is an ideal candidate for the photocatalytic degradation of MB and exhibit a maximum degradation efficiency of 95% in a neutral medium under UV-light irradiation for 60min. More importantly, the ZnO@N-C hybrid composite with the nitrogen and oxygenated carbon functional groups originated from the environmental-friendly natural green source. Hence, this method of synthesized carbon-based metal oxide photocatalyst could be an ideal alternative candidate than the other hazardous graphene-based metal oxides. In addition, the high-quality water soluble fluorescent N-CDs resulted from the filtrate of synthesized ZnO@N-C hybrid composite. The obtained N-CDs is about 4nm with a narrow size distribution and their aqueous solution emits the multicolour fluorescence without any surface passivation or oxidization by varying the excitation wavelength. Further studies showed that the N-CDs can be used as a fluorescent probe for multicolour cellular imaging with excellent biocompatibility. Therefore, our results confirm that the ZnO@N-C hybrid composite could be an excellent photocatalyst for the degradation of MB dye pollutants, the N-CDs could be a multicolour fluorescence probe for cellular imaging in the real biological system, clinical application and thus can be a suitable candidate for eco-friendly environmental applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Nano Material Technology Development Program of the Korean National Research Foundation (NRF) funded by the Korean Ministry of Education, Science, and Technology (grant number 2012M3A7B4049675). This work was also supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Priority Research Centers Program (grant number 2014R1A6A1031189).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2017.09.038.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
